SP26 Data Analysis

Kaylee Fernandez

2026-05-11

Method

Participants

19 participants completed the experiment: 10 L1 Spanish speakers and 9 L2 Spanish learners.

Procedure

Three-phase Spanish stress-suffix association task in PsychoPy: pre-test (360 trials, 6 blocks), training (120 trials with feedback), post-test (360 trials, parallel structure). On each trial, participants heard a spoken stimulus and selected PRESENTE or PASADO.

Pre/post stimuli were drawn from six conditions varying in verb stress (paroxytone, oxytone), noun match (Match, Mismatch), and verb match (Match, Mismatch). Training stimuli were two conditions: parox (a) and oxy (b).

Table 1. Condition coding for pre- and post-test stimuli.
Cond Name Verb stress Verb match Suffix tense Noun match Splice
a parox_nounmatch_verbmatch paroxytone Match present Match a
b oxy_nounmismatch_verbmatch oxytone Match past Mismatch a
c parox_nounmatch_verbmismatch paroxytone Mismatch past Match a
d parox_nounmismatch_verbmatch paroxytone Match present Mismatch c
e oxy_nounmatch_verbmismatch oxytone Mismatch present Match c
f oxy_nounmatch_verbmatch oxytone Match past Match c
Table 2. Condition coding for training stimuli.
Cond Name Verb stress Tense
a parox paroxytone present
b oxy oxytone past

Summary of Findings

General Description

Accuracy and RT data from the pre- and post-test phases were analyzed using generalized linear mixed-effects models in R with the lme4 package. Accuracy (binary correct/incorrect) was modeled with a binomial family and logit link. RT on correct trials (200–5000 ms) was modeled with a Gaussian family on the 1/RT (reciprocal/speed) transformation, selected via Box-Cox analysis (optimal λ ≈ −0.7; 95% CI [−0.8, −0.6]). Training-phase accuracy was modeled with a binomial-logit growth-curve GLMM with standardized trial number as a continuous predictor. All models included group (L1, L2), phase (pre, post), their interaction, and condition (6 levels) as fixed effects; the training model included group, trial_z, and their interaction. All categorical predictors used sum-to-zero contrasts so that coefficients represent deviations from the grand mean. Random-effects structure: by-participant intercepts and slopes for phase (or trial in the training model) and by-item intercepts. Model assumptions were assessed with the DHARMa package using scaled quantile residuals; QQ-plots and dispersion tests were used to evaluate fit. Statistical significance of main effects and interactions was assessed via nested model comparisons; main effects involved in interactions were tested against a no-interaction model to preserve marginality. Marginal and conditional R² were calculated following Nakagawa and Schielzeth (2013). Decision rule: α = .05.

Results

The accuracy model was fit with a maximal error term (marginal R² = 0.04; conditional R² = 0.38). The analysis revealed a significant group × phase interaction [χ²(1) = 4.62; p = .032] and a significant main effect of condition [χ²(5) = 15.39; p = .009]. The main effects of group [χ²(1) = 2.94; p = .087] and phase [χ²(1) = 0.01; p = .944] were not significant. The RT model was fit with a maximal error term (marginal R² = 0.10; conditional R² = 0.32). The analysis revealed significant main effects of group [χ²(1) = 6.27; p = .012], phase [χ²(1) = 22.06; p < .001], and condition [χ²(5) = 119.38; p < .001]; the group × phase interaction was not significant [χ²(1) = 2.92; p = .087]. The training-phase model (marginal R² = 0.05; conditional R² = 0.19) showed a significant main effect of group [χ²(1) = 6.73; p = .009]; the effects of trial [χ²(1) = 2.86; p = .091] and group × trial [χ²(1) = 0.55; p = .458] were not significant.

Four follow-up analyses examined the verb-mismatch and noun-mismatch effects on accuracy and RT. The verb-match accuracy model revealed no significant interactions of group × verb match [χ²(1) = 2.82; p = .093], phase × verb match [χ²(1) = 0.78; p = .379], or group × phase × verb match [χ²(1) = 0.02; p = .899]. The verb-match RT model revealed significant interactions of group × verb match [χ²(1) = 9.43; p = .002] and phase × verb match [χ²(1) = 4.94; p = .026]; the three-way interaction was not significant [χ²(1) = 0.33; p = .565]. The noun-match models revealed no significant effects in either accuracy or RT (all p > .12). The verb-stress × verb-match accuracy model revealed a significant main effect of verb stress [χ²(1) = 4.89; p = .027] and verb match [χ²(1) = 8.54; p = .003]; the interaction was not significant [χ²(1) = 0.01; p = .930]. The verb-stress × verb-match RT model revealed significant main effects of verb stress [χ²(1) = 45.98; p < .001] and verb match [χ²(1) = 14.00; p < .001], and a significant verb-stress × verb-match interaction [χ²(1) = 4.92; p = .026]. The group × verb-stress × verb-match accuracy model revealed no significant effects (all p > .05). The group × verb-stress × verb-match RT model revealed a significant three-way interaction [χ²(1) = 15.21; p < .001], a significant group × verb match interaction [χ²(1) = 9.11; p = .003], and a significant verb-stress × verb-match interaction [χ²(1) = 4.94; p = .026]; the group × verb-stress interaction was not significant [χ²(1) = 2.41; p = .121].

Model-predicted accuracy by group and phase.

Effects on accuracy (NMC):

  • Group: χ²(1) = 2.94, p = 0.086
  • Phase: χ²(1) = 0.01, p = 0.944
  • Group × Phase: χ²(1) = 4.62, p = 0.032
  • Condition: χ²(5) = 15.39, p = 0.009

Direction: L2 accuracy increased from pre to post; L1 accuracy was stable. Condition main effect present.

Effects on RT (NMC):

  • Phase: χ²(1) = 22.06, p < .001
  • Group × Phase: χ²(1) = 2.92, p = 0.087

Direction: L1 faster than L2 overall. Both groups faster at post than at pre. Condition main effect present.

Effects on training accuracy (NMC):

  • Trial: χ²(1) = 2.86, p = 0.091
  • Group × Trial: χ²(1) = 0.55, p = 0.458

Direction: L1 higher accuracy than L2 throughout training. Trial effect not significant. No group × trial interaction.

Forest Plot of Primary Fixed Effects

Fixed-effect estimates from the three primary models with 95% CIs. Condition main effects omitted.

Follow-Up: Verb- and Noun-Mismatch Effects

Follow-up analyses examined the verb-mismatch and noun-mismatch effects on accuracy and RT. Four pairs of models test (a) group × phase × verb_match, (b) group × phase × noun_match, (c) verb_stress × verb_match, and (d) group × verb_stress × verb_match. Mismatch cost convention: positive cost = mismatch worse than match (= prediction surprise). RT cost = Mismatch − Match (ms); accuracy cost = Match − Mismatch (percentage points).

Verb match × group × phase

Model-predicted RT by verb match, group, and phase.

Model-predicted accuracy by verb match, group, and phase.

Model-derived RT mismatch cost by group and phase. Positive = mismatch slower than match.

Model-derived accuracy mismatch cost by group and phase. Positive = mismatch less accurate than match.

Accuracy effects (NMC):

  • Group × verb match: χ²(1) = 2.82, p = 0.093
  • Phase × verb match: χ²(1) = 0.78, p = 0.379
  • Group × Phase × verb match: χ²(1) = 0.02, p = 0.899

RT effects (NMC):

  • Group × verb match: χ²(1) = 9.43, p = 0.002
  • Phase × verb match: χ²(1) = 4.94, p = 0.026
  • Group × Phase × verb match: χ²(1) = 0.33, p = 0.565

Direction (RT): L1 showed a larger verb-mismatch cost than L2 overall. Verb-mismatch cost was larger at post than at pre. The pattern of growth from pre to post was similar in both groups.

  • Verb stress: χ²(1) = 45.98, p < .001
  • Verb match: χ²(1) = 14.00, p < .001
  • Verb stress × verb match: χ²(1) = 4.92, p = 0.026

Direction (RT): Verb-mismatch cost was larger for paroxytone stimuli (~46 ms) than for oxytone stimuli (~14 ms).

acc_g_s_vm group:verb_stress:verb_match 0.01 1 0.931

Accuracy effects (NMC):

  • Group × verb stress: χ²(1) = 0.01, p = 0.903
  • Group × verb match: χ²(1) = 3.80, p = 0.051
  • Verb stress × verb match: χ²(1) = 0.00, p = 0.954
  • Group × verb stress × verb match: χ²(1) = 0.01, p = 0.931

RT effects (NMC):

  • Group × verb stress: χ²(1) = 2.41, p = 0.121
  • Group × verb match: χ²(1) = 9.11, p = 0.003
  • Verb stress × verb match: χ²(1) = 4.94, p = 0.026
  • Group × verb stress × verb match: χ²(1) = 15.21, p < .001

Direction (RT): L1 speakers showed positive verb-mismatch costs on both paroxytone (~41 ms) and oxytone (~54 ms) stimuli. L2 learners showed a positive cost on paroxytone (~70 ms) and a negative cost on oxytone (−51 ms).

Diagnostics

DHARMa diagnostic summary. Dispersion p > .05 = adequate variance fit. KS p-values are oversensitive at large n; QQ-plots were inspected visually.

dispersion…2 rt 0.000 0.949 0.532
dispersion…3 training 0.316 1.010 0.880
